revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Keijō Gokoku Shrine | image = | location = {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, Korea | coordinates = {{Coord|37|32|10|N|126|58|45|E|region:KR_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | deity = The deities enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, limited to those with connections to Korea outside {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. | classification = Designated Gokoku Shrine by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} | established = October 23, 1943 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 18) | annual_festival = October 11 }}
'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, in what is now {{ill|Seoul|ja|ソウル特別市}}, {{ill|South Korea|ja|大韓民国}}. The shrine enshrined the same deities as {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. It was classified as a designated {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}.
== History ==
From {{ill|1885|ja|1885年}} ({{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}} 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} was erected in {{ill|Namsan|ja|南山}}, after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By {{ill|1905|ja|1905年}} (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General {{ill|Minami Jirō|ja|南次郎}}, the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.
== Enshrined Deities ==
In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Sources == * {{Citation|ja|author = 青井哲人|title = 神社造営よりみた日本植民地の環境変容に関する研究 : 朝鮮を事例として|year = 2000|ref = kaigai}} * {{Cite journal |ja |author= 佐藤弘樹|title= 資料紹介 戦前の海外神社一覧(2)朝鮮・関東州・満州国・中華民国 |journal= 神社本廳教學研究所紀要|year= 1998|pages= 133–200|ref= satou}} * {{Citation|ja|author = 朝鮮総督府|title = 施政三十年史|publisher = 朝鮮総督府|year = 1940|url = {{NDLDC|1445153}}|ref = shisei}} * {{Citation|ja|author = 京城日報社|title = 朝鮮年鑑 昭和20年版|publisher = 京城日報社|year = 1944|url = {{NDLDC|1708339}}|ref = Chosun}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Keijō Gokoku Shrine | image = | location = {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, Korea | coordinates = {{Coord|37|32|10|N|126|58|45|E|region:KR_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | deity = The deities enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, limited to those with connections to Korea outside {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. | classification = Designated Gokoku Shrine by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} | established = October 23, 1943 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 18) | annual_festival = October 11 }}
'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, in what is now {{ill|Seoul|ja|ソウル特別市}}, {{ill|South Korea|ja|大韓民国}}. The shrine enshrined the same deities as {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. It was classified as a designated {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}.
== History ==
From {{ill|1885|ja|1885年}} ({{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}} 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} was erected in {{ill|Namsan|ja|南山}}, after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By {{ill|1905|ja|1905年}} (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General {{ill|Minami Jirō|ja|南次郎}}, the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.
== Enshrined Deities ==
In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Keijō Gokoku Shrine | image = | location = {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, Korea | coordinates = {{Coord|37|32|10|N|126|58|45|E|region:KR_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | deity = The deities enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, limited to those with connections to Korea outside {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. | classification = Designated Gokoku Shrine by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} | established = October 23, 1943 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 18) | annual_festival = October 11 }}
'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, in what is now {{ill|Seoul|ja|ソウル特別市}}, {{ill|South Korea|ja|大韓民国}}. The shrine enshrined the same deities as {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. It was classified as a designated {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}.
== History ==
From {{ill|1885|ja|1885年}} ({{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}} 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} was erected in {{ill|Namsan|ja|南山}}, after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By {{ill|1905|ja|1905年}} (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General {{ill|Minami Jirō|ja|南次郎}}, the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.
== Enshrined Deities ==
In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General {{ill|Minami Jirō|ja|南次郎}}, the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.
== Enshrined Deities ==
In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Keijō Gokoku Shrine | image = | location = {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, Korea | coordinates = {{Coord|37|32|10|N|126|58|45|E|region:KR_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | deity = The deities enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, limited to those with connections to Korea outside {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. | classification = Designated Gokoku Shrine by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} | established = October 23, 1943 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 18) | annual_festival = October 11 }}
'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, in what is now {{ill|Seoul|ja|ソウル特別市}}, {{ill|South Korea|ja|大韓民国}}. The shrine enshrined the same deities as {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. It was classified as a designated {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}.
== History ==
From {{ill|1885|ja|1885th year }} ({{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}} 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year }} (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} was erected in {{ill|Namsan|ja|南山}}, after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By {{ill|1905|ja|1905th year }} (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General {{ill|Minami Jirō|ja|南次郎}}, the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.

In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

In {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year }} (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} was erected in {{ill|Namsan|ja|南山}}, after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By {{ill|1905|ja|1905th year }} (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General {{ill|Minami Jirō|ja|南次郎}}, the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.
== Enshrined Deities ==
In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Sources == * {{Citation|ja|author = 青井哲人|title = 神社造営よりみた日本植民地の環境変容に関する研究 : 朝鮮を事例として|year = 2000|ref = kaigai}} * {{Cite journal |ja |author= 佐藤弘樹|title= 資料紹介 戦前の海外神社一覧(2)朝鮮・関東州・満州国・中華民国 |journal= 神社本廳教學研究所紀要|year= 1998|pages= 133–200|ref= satou}} * {{Citation|ja|author = 朝鮮総督府|title = 施政三十年史|publisher = 朝鮮総督府|year = 1940|url = {{NDLDC|1445153}}|ref = shisei}} * {{Citation|ja|author = 京城日報社|title = 朝鮮年鑑 昭和20th year 版|publisher = 京城日報社|year = 1944|url = {{NDLDC|1708339}}|ref = Chosun}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Keijō Gokoku Shrine | image = | location = {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, Korea | coordinates = {{Coord|37|32|10|N|126|58|45|E|region:KR_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | deity = The deities enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, limited to those with connections to Korea outside {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. | classification = Designated Gokoku Shrine by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} | established = October 23, 1943 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 18) | annual_festival = October 11 }}
'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区}}, in what is now {{ill|Seoul|ja|ソウル特別市}}, {{ill|South Korea|ja|大韓民国}}. The shrine enshrined the same deities as {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}, specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}}. It was classified as a designated {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}}.
== History ==
From {{ill|1885|ja|1885th year }} ({{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治}} 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year }} (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争}} was erected in {{ill|Namsan|ja|南山}}, after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By {{ill|1905|ja|1905th year }} (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和}} 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General {{ill|Minami Jirō|ja|南次郎}}, the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.
== Enshrined Deities ==
In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Keijō Gokoku Shrine | image = | location = {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区|12=draft}}, Korea | coordinates = {{Coord|37|32|10|N|126|58|45|E|region:KR_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | deity = The deities enshrined at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社|12=draft}}, limited to those with connections to Korea outside {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道|12=draft}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道|12=draft}}. | classification = Designated Gokoku Shrine by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|12=draft}} | established = October 23, 1943 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 18) | annual_festival = October 11 }}
'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京城府|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区|12=draft}}, in what is now {{ill|Seoul|ja|ソウル特別市|12=draft}}, {{ill|South Korea|ja|大韓民国|12=draft}}. The shrine enshrined the same deities as {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社|12=draft}}, specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道|12=draft}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道|12=draft}}. It was classified as a designated {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|12=draft}}.
== History ==
From {{ill|1885|ja|1885th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|12=draft}} 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|ja|日清戦争|12=draft}} was erected in {{ill|Namsan|ja|南山|12=draft}}, after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By {{ill|1905|ja|1905th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ({{ill|Shōwa era|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|12=draft}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南|12=draft}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津|12=draft}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General {{ill|Minami Jirō|ja|南次郎|12=draft}}, the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍|12=draft}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.
== Enshrined Deities ==
In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * {{ill|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)|ja|江原道|12=draft}}, {{ill|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡南道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|咸鏡北道|12=draft}} were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)|ja|京畿道|12=draft}}, {{ill|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清北道|12=draft}}, {{ill|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)|ja|忠清南道|12=draft}}, {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|12=draft}}, {{ill|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅南道|12=draft}}, {{ill|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安北道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)|ja|平安南道|12=draft}} were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Sources == * {{Citation|ja|author = 青井哲人|title = 神社造営よりみた日本植民地の環境変容に関する研究 : 朝鮮を事例として|year = 2000|ref = kaigai}} * {{Cite journal |ja |author= 佐藤弘樹|title= 資料紹介 戦前の海外神社一覧(2)朝鮮・関東州・満州国・中華民国 |journal= 神社本廳教學研究所紀要|year= 1998|pages= 133–200|ref= satou}} * {{Citation|ja|author = 朝鮮総督府|title = 施政三十年史|publisher = 朝鮮総督府|year = 1940|url = {{NDLDC|1445153}}|ref = shisei}} * {{Citation|ja|author = 京城日報社|title = 朝鮮年鑑 昭和20th year 版|publisher = 京城日報社|year = 1944|url = {{NDLDC|1708339}}|ref = Chosun}}

'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Keijō|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzan District, Seoul|qq=}}, in what is now [[:en:Seoul|Seoul]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. The shrine enshrined the same deities as [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]], specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]]. It was classified as a designated [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|qq=}}.
== History ==
From [[:en:1885|1885]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In [[:en:1889|1889]] (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]] was erected in [[:en:Namsan|Namsan]], after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|qq=}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 羅南区域}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chongjin|qq=}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General [[:en:Jirō Minami|Minami Jirō]], the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korea Army (Japanese Army)|qq=}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.
== Enshrined Deities ==
In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.
Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.
The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. * [[:en:Gyeonggi Province|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Chungcheong Province|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Chungcheong Province|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 全北特別自治道}}, [[:en:South Jeolla Province|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Pyongan Province|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:South Pyongan Province|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.
== Postwar Period ==
After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Keijō|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzan District, Seoul|qq=}}, in what is now [[:en:Seoul|Seoul]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. The shrine enshrined the same deities as [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]], specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]]. It was classified as a designated [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|qq=}}.

In [[:en:1889|1889]] (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]] was erected in [[:en:Namsan|Namsan]], after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.
Following the 1939 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|qq=}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 羅南区域}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chongjin|qq=}}).
Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General [[:en:Jirō Minami|Minami Jirō]], the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korea Army (Japanese Army)|qq=}}, and 500 others. The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation: * [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. * [[:en:Gyeonggi Province|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Chungcheong Province|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Chungcheong Province|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 全北特別自治道}}, [[:en:South Jeolla Province|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Pyongan Province|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:South Pyongan Province|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.

'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Keijō|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzan District, Seoul|qq=|1=ryuusanku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1208605}}, in what is now [[:en:Seoul|Seoul]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. The shrine enshrined the same deities as [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]], specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]]. It was classified as a designated [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|qq=|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}}.

== History ==

From [[:en:1885|1885]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In [[:en:1889|1889]] (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]] was erected in [[:en:Namsan|Namsan]], after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.

Following the 1939 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|qq=|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 羅南区域|1=Ranam-guyok|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3916308}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chongjin|qq=|1=Chongjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561255}}). 

Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General [[:en:Jirō Minami|Minami Jirō]], the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korea Army (Japanese Army)|qq=|1=Korea Army (Japanese Army)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16182072}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.

== Enshrined Deities ==

In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.

Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.

The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation:
* [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135491645}}.
* [[:en:Gyeonggi Province|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Chungcheong Province|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Chungcheong Province|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 全北特別自治道|1=North Jeolla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q41157}}, [[:en:South Jeolla Province|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Pyongan Province|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:South Pyongan Province|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.

== Postwar Period ==

After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

It was classified as a designated [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|qq=|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}}.

== History ==

From [[:en:1885|1885]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In [[:en:1889|1889]] (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]] was erected in [[:en:Namsan|Namsan]], after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.

Following the 1939 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|qq=|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 羅南区域|1=Ranam-guyok|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3916308}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chongjin|qq=|1=Chongjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561255}}). 

Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General [[:en:Jirō Minami|Minami Jirō]], the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korea Army (Japanese Army)|qq=|1=Korea Army (Japanese Army)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16182072}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.

== Enshrined Deities ==

In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.

Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.

The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation:
* [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491645}}.
* [[:en:Gyeonggi Province|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Chungcheong Province|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Chungcheong Province|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 全北特別自治道|1=North Jeolla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q41157}}, [[:en:South Jeolla Province|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Pyongan Province|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:South Pyongan Province|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.

== Postwar Period ==

After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

| established = October 23, 1943 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] 18)

'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Keijō|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzan District, Seoul|1=ryuusanku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1208605}}, in what is now [[:en:Seoul|Seoul]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. The shrine enshrined the same deities as [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]], specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]]. It was classified as a designated [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}}.

== History ==

From [[:en:1885|1885]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In [[:en:1889|1889]] (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]] was erected in [[:en:Namsan|Namsan]], after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.

Following the 1939 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 羅南区域|1=Ranam-guyok|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3916308}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chongjin|1=Chongjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561255}}). 

Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General [[:en:Jirō Minami|Minami Jirō]], the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korea Army (Japanese Army)|1=Korea Army (Japanese Army)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16182072}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.

== Enshrined Deities ==

In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.

Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.

The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation:
* [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491645}}.
* [[:en:Gyeonggi Province|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Chungcheong Province|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Chungcheong Province|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 全北特別自治道|1=North Jeolla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q41157}}, [[:en:South Jeolla Province|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Pyongan Province|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:South Pyongan Province|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.

== Postwar Period ==

After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Keijō|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzan District, Seoul|1=ryuusanku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1208605}}, in what is now [[:en:Seoul|Seoul]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. The shrine enshrined the same deities as [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]], specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]]. It was classified as a designated [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}}.

== History ==

From [[:en:1885|1885]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In [[:en:1889|1889]] (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]] was erected in [[:en:Namsan|Namsan]], after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.

Following the 1939 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 羅南区域|1=Ranam-guyok|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3916308}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chongjin|1=Chongjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561255}}). 

Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General [[:en:Jirō Minami|Minami Jirō]], the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korea Army (Japanese Army)|1=Korea Army (Japanese Army)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16182072}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.

== Enshrined Deities ==

In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.

Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.

The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation:
* [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491645}}.
* [[:en:Gyeonggi Province|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Chungcheong Province|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Chungcheong Province|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 全北特別自治道|1=North Jeolla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q41157}}, [[:en:South Jeolla Province|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Pyongan Province|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:South Pyongan Province|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.

== Postwar Period ==

After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

'''Keijō Gokoku Shrine''' (京城護国神社, ''Keijō Gokoku Jinja'') was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Keijō|Gyeongseong, Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|Ryūzan District, Seoul|ja|龍山区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzan District, Seoul|1=ryuusanku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1208605}}, in what is now [[:en:Seoul|Seoul]], [[:en:South Korea|South Korea]]. The shrine enshrined the same deities as [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]], specifically those with connections to Korea, excluding individuals from [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]]. It was classified as a designated [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] by the {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}}.

== History ==

From [[:en:1885|1885]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 16), the Japanese residents of Gyeongseong held memorial ceremonies for the war dead in private residences. In [[:en:1889|1889]] (Meiji 22), a victory monument for the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]] was erected in [[:en:Namsan|Namsan]], after which memorial ceremonies were conducted there. By [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), these ceremonies began aligning with Yasukuni Shrine's annual festivals.

Following the 1939 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] 14) renaming of local Shōkonsha (招魂社) to Gokoku Shrines across Japan, efforts emerged in Korea to establish similar shrines. By the end of 1937 (Shōwa 12), Yasukuni Shrine had enshrined 5,000 spirits from Korea, prompting public and official calls for a dedicated Gokoku Shrine in Korea. The {{ill|Governor-General of Korea|ja|朝鮮総督府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor-General of Korea|1=Government-General of Chōsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125097168}} approved the construction of two shrines: one in Keijō (Gyeongseong) and another in {{ill|Ranan|ja|羅南|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 羅南区域|1=Ranam-guyok|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3916308}} (now part of {{ill|Chongjin|ja|清津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chongjin|1=Chongjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561255}}). 

Construction funding included 1.5 million yen from the national treasury and 8 million yen in local donations. The shrine site was selected on the southwestern foothills of Namsan. Construction began in August 1940 (Shōwa 15), utilizing student labor brigades. On October 26, a groundbreaking ceremony was attended by Governor-General [[:en:Jirō Minami|Minami Jirō]], the commander of the {{ill|Korea Army (Japanese Army)|ja|朝鮮軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korea Army (Japanese Army)|1=Korea Army (Japanese Army)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16182072}}, and 500 others. The shrine was completed in autumn 1943 (Shōwa 18) and held its enshrinement ceremony on October 23.

== Enshrined Deities ==

In contrast to mainland Japan, where each prefecture had its own Gokoku Shrine for local war dead, Korea had fewer military divisions and no existing Shōkonsha. Instead, the authorities established only two shrines—one in Keijō and another in Ranan—to cover the entire Korean Peninsula.

Keijō Gokoku Shrine enshrined individuals with permanent residence in Korea, those who died in military service while stationed in Korea, and individuals affiliated with military units, naval vessels, or government offices in Korea who were already enshrined at Yasukuni Shrine. Individuals with domicile in mainland Japan were enshrined at three locations: Yasukuni Shrine, their home prefecture's Gokoku Shrine, and either Keijō or Ranan Gokoku Shrine, depending on the regional jurisdiction.

The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation:
* [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491645}}.
* [[:en:Gyeonggi Province|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Chungcheong Province|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Chungcheong Province|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 全北特別自治道|1=North Jeolla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q41157}}, [[:en:South Jeolla Province|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Pyongan Province|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:South Pyongan Province|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.

== Postwar Period ==

After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.

The division between Keijō and Ranan Gokoku Shrines was based on domicile, residence, and unit affiliation:
* [[:en:Gangwon|Gangwon Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Hamgyong Province|South Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:North Hamgyong Province|North Hamgyong Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to {{ill|Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja|羅南護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranan Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491645}}.
* [[:en:Gyeonggi Province|Gyeonggi Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Chungcheong Province|North Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:South Chungcheong Province|South Chungcheong Province (Japanese rule)]], {{ill|North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja|全羅北道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 全北特別自治道|1=North Jeolla|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q41157}}, [[:en:South Jeolla Province|South Jeolla Province (Japanese rule)]], [[:en:North Pyongan Province|North Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]], and [[:en:South Pyongan Province|South Pyongan Province (Japanese rule)]] were assigned to Keijō Gokoku Shrine.

== Postwar Period ==

After Japan’s defeat in World War II, the shrine was abandoned, and Korean refugees from North Korea began settling in the area. Although little remains of the original shrine, a staircase of 108 steps survived and became known as the "108 Stairs," a famous historical landmark in the area.
